coredata.dc:title: Optical, microstructural, and magnetic hyperthermia properties of green-synthesized Fe3O4/carbon dots nanocomposites utilizing Moringa oleifera extract and watermelon rinds

coredata.dc:description: Cancer therapy with targeted-localized heating is one of the breakthroughs in the biomedical field. The incorporation of magnetic nanoparticles and fluorescent nanoparticles was one of the crucial issues for magnetic hyperthermia applications. This research investigates the magnetic hyperthermia properties of green-synthesized Fe3O4/CDs. Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized using the coprecipitation method with Moringa oleifera leaf extract as a reducing agent and stabilizer. In contrast, CDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method with watermelon rind waste as a carbon source. X-ray diffraction analysis confirmed the presence of cubic inverse spinel and a reduction in crystallite size with increasing CDs concentration. Transmission electron microscopy revealed particle size distributions of 9.7 nm for Fe3O4 and 7.5 nm for Fe3O4/CDs. Scanning electron microscopy showed that CDs were distributed on the surface of Fe3O4. The detection of characteristic FeO, CC, CO, and CO-C bonds indicated the presence of CDs on the surface of Fe3O4. Ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy spectra absorption peaks at 282 nm for CDs and 193 nm for Fe3O4. Photoluminescence spectra indicated shifts from 509 to 505 nm in excitation wavelength for both CDs and Fe3O4/CDs, situated within the green region of visible light. The vibrating sample magnetometer revealed that the nanocomposites displayed characteristics of soft ferromagnetic materials. Furthermore, the specific absorption rate (SAR) value of Fe3O4/CDs was found to be dependent on magnetization. The SAR value of Fe3O4/CDs decreased as the concentration of CDs increased, and the frequency and strength of the AMF increased. Therefore, these results can promote Fe3O4/CDs nanocomposites as a promising candidate for magnetic hyperthermia applications.

head.ce:title: Optical, microstructural, and magnetic hyperthermia properties of green-synthesized Fe3O4/carbon dots nanocomposites utilizing Moringa oleifera extract and watermelon rinds

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cancer therapy with targeted-localized heating is one of the breakthroughs in the biomedical field. The incorporation of magnetic nanoparticles and fluorescent nanoparticles was one of the crucial issues for magnetic hyperthermia applications. This research investigates the magnetic hyperthermia properties of green-synthesized Fe3O4/CDs. Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized using the coprecipitation method with Moringa oleifera leaf extract as a reducing agent and stabilizer. In contrast, CDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method with watermelon rind waste as a carbon source. X-ray diffraction analysis confirmed the presence of cubic inverse spinel and a reduction in crystallite size with increasing CDs concentration. Transmission electron microscopy revealed particle size distributions of 9.7 nm for Fe3O4 and 7.5 nm for Fe3O4/CDs. Scanning electron microscopy showed that CDs were distributed on the surface of Fe3O4. The detection of characteristic FeO, CC, CO, and CO-C bonds indicated the presence of CDs on the surface of Fe3O4. Ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy spectra absorption peaks at 282 nm for CDs and 193 nm for Fe3O4. Photoluminescence spectra indicated shifts from 509 to 505 nm in excitation wavelength for both CDs and Fe3O4/CDs, situated within the green region of visible light. The vibrating sample magnetometer revealed that the nanocomposites displayed characteristics of soft ferromagnetic materials. Furthermore, the specific absorption rate (SAR) value of Fe3O4/CDs was found to be dependent on magnetization. The SAR value of Fe3O4/CDs decreased as the concentration of CDs increased, and the frequency and strength of the AMF increased. Therefore, these results can promote Fe3O4/CDs nanocomposites as a promising candidate for magnetic hyperthermia applications.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomedicine, which combines nanoscale technology and biology, is a promising and fascinating field of research for the future [1]. It aims to create more effective and accurate medical therapies and treatments. The biomedical field has shown a significant interest in the potential applications of nanocomposites due to their unique combination of magnetic and luminescence properties [2,3]. The various properties of these materials enable their use in a wide range of applications, including targeted drug delivery, magnetic resonance imaging, disease diagnosis, bio-separation, magnetic hyperthermia for cancer treatment, and magnetic storage media [2].

Magnetic hyperthermia is a treatment that uses an alternating field (AMF) on nanoscale magnetic materials to generate heat within the tumor area to treat cancer [4–8]. Nanoscale technology ensures that the magnetic particles are only concentrated in the tumor area. In the magnetic hyperthermia treatment, fluorescent nanoparticles were used for evaluating doses and detecting the precise position of the magnetic nanoparticles. Then, an AMF is applied to the area to induce heat and kill cancer cells. The heating mechanism in magnetic hyperthermia is caused by Brownian and Nèel relaxation, hysteresis loss, and eddy currents [9,10]. Measuring the effectiveness of heat generation in magnetic hyperthermia involves determining the specific absorption rate (SAR), which depends on various factors. These factors include the size, shape, magnetic anisotropy, saturation magnetization, magnetic properties of the nanoparticles, frequency, and strength of the AMF [11,12]. The SAR value must be high enough to ensure the heat generated can kill cancer cells around 42–45 °C [13,14]. However, SAR values that are too high can also be harmful to healthy body tissues, so the magnetic field and magnetic particle settings must be optimized to ensure maximum effectiveness and safety [15,16].

Magnetic materials, such as Fe3O4 [2,4,17–19], ZnFe2O4 [20], CoFe2O4 [21], and CuFe2O4 [22], are used for the development of magnetic hyperthermia applications. There were chosen for their excellent biocompatibility and magnetic properties. Among other materials Fe3O4 nanoparticles, have shown promise as suitable materials due to their high saturation magnetization, superparamagnetic properties, large surface area, high biocompatibility, and low toxicity [5,17,18]. The saturation magnetization was also proportional to the heating efficiency for magnetic hyperthermia, with high saturation magnetization capacities are desired in order to apply lower AMF during magnetic hyperthermia. The distinctive physical and chemical properties, biocompatibility, and customizable magnetic characteristics make these nanoparticles appropriate for medical use as magnetic hyperthermia agents [2].

To improve the main characteristics of magnetic hyperthermia therapy, fluorescent materials should be added for evaluating doses and detecting the precise position of the magnetic nanoparticles. Coatings using ZnO [21], ZnS [2], and carbon dots (CDs) [23] have been utilized to enhance the luminescence feature of magnetic hyperthermia. Among these materials, CDs possess excellent qualities such as high water solubility, chemical stability, non-toxicity, easy modification, biocompatibility, and resistance to photobleaching [20,21]. CDs are employed as a coating to improve their luminescence features. CDs are carbon materials with no defined structure and zero dimensionality, having an average size of less than 10 nm [24]. CDs are synthesized using top-down and bottom-up methods. Top-down methods break down materials like graphite and active carbon into CDs using laser ablation, arc discharge, and electrochemical techniques. Bottom-up methods synthesize CDs from precursor molecules of varying sizes, such as carbohydrates, organic acids, and natural products, using solvothermal, hydrothermal, chemical, and microwave synthesis [25]. The hydrothermal method offers a straightforward and eco-friendly approach to creating CDs, which is cost-effective and delivers consistent particle sizes [26]. The incorporation of Fe3O4 with carbon dots can be done by stirring, hydrothermal, and sonication methods [27–30]. Previous studies used conventional methods that were not environmentally friendly, cost-effective, and caused long-term effects on human health [23,24].

Green synthesis methods are being developed to overcome the limitations of conventional methods. Green synthesis, which utilizes plant extracts to make nanoparticles, offers several benefits, such as reducing toxic waste products, ensuring environmental non-toxicity and safety, and being cost-effective and efficient [26,31–34]. Moringa oleifera (MO) is a commonly used starting material for the green synthesis of nanoparticles due to its high concentration of phytochemical substances, such as phenolics and flavonoids. These substances act as both reducing agents and stabilizers during the synthesis of nanoparticles due to their abundance in MO leaves [35]. Furthermore, MO contains abundant cationic proteins that exhibit coagulant properties, enabling it to efficiently purify surfaces and precipitate organic or mineral particles in a solution [36]. Additionally, the rinds of fruits and discarded materials are frequently utilized as eco-friendly precursors for producing CDs [37–39].

Our recent study successfully utilized MO leaf extract to synthesize environmentally friendly Fe3O4 nanoparticles [40–43]. Additionally, we have reported the potential applications of bismuth ferrite/SiO2 as hyperthermia magnetic agents [15], and Sari et al. have studied Fe3O4/CDs nanocomposite with superparamagnetic ability and also has enough electrons to act as a photocatalyst [42]. Using magnetic and fluorescent properties in nanocomposites has revolutionized biomedical research, providing new opportunities for cancer treatment. As reported by Mondal [2], Fe3O4/ZnS composite nanoparticles have shown promising results in applying magnetic hyperthermia with moderate SAR. However, previous studies have not reported the magnetic hyperthermia potential of Fe3O4/CDs nanocomposites. On the other hand, the green-synthesized nanocomposites as magnetic hyperthermia agents for cancer treatment have not yet been fully investigated. Further research is necessary to explore the potential of green-synthesized nanocomposites. Therefore, this study focused on the successful synthesis of Fe3O4/CDs nanocomposites using a green synthesis method, presenting a promising opportunity for magnetic hyperthermia application to cancer treatment. The optical, microstructural, and magnetic properties of the green synthesized Fe3O4/CDs nanocomposites were also studied to obtain the comprehensive study.

The MO leaf extract used in the study was obtained from Timur Rasa Indonesia, while the watermelon rinds waste was obtained from a local market in Indonesia. Iron (II) sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O), iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3.6H2O), 25 % ammonia (NH4OH) solution, 96 % ethanol (CH3CH2OH), and distilled water were used as other precursors. All chemicals used were of analytical grade and did not require further purification, and were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, GFR). The aim was to investigate the optical, magnetic, morphological, and structural properties of Fe3O4/CDs, and to evaluate their potential for use in magnetic hyperthermia through SAR measurements.

Preparation of MO Solution: To create the MO solution, 5 g of MO extract were dissolved in 60 mL of distilled water at 60 °C. After stirring for one hour, the solution was allowed to cool to room temperature and subsequently filtered using Whatman 01 paper. The resulting MO solution was stored in a refrigerator, as illustrated in Fig. 1(a) .

Green Synthesis of Fe3O4: The Fe3O4 was obtained using the co-precipitation method, as described in previous studies [35,44]. Initially, a mixture of 4.054 g of FeCl3·6H2O and 2.086 g of FeSO4·7H2O was dissolved in distilled water and stirred for 15 min. Subsequently, 10 mL of the MO solution was added to the mixture while stirring at 600 rpm, followed by the dropwise addition of a 10 % NH4OH solution for 90 min at 60 °C. The precipitate was then magnetically separated, washed with distilled water, and dried at 100 °C for 2 h. This process is illustrated in Fig. 1.(b).

Green Synthesis of CDs: This research obtained CDs using the hydrothermal method with watermelon rind waste [42], as shown in Fig. 1(c). The watermelon rinds juice was mixed with distilled water and ethanol 96 % in a 1:1:1 ratio and stirred for 30 min. Next, the hydrothermal autoclave reactor was used to heat the solution at 180 °C for 3 h. The solution was then filtered using Whatman 01 paper at room temperature and stored in a refrigerator for future use.

Fabrication of Fe3O4/CDs Nanocomposites: In Fig. 1(d), the fabrication process of Fe3O4/CDs is shown. To prepare this nanocomposite, 0.8 g of Fe3O4 were dissolved in 10 mL of CDs solution and then sonicated for 30 min. Over a 24 h incubation period, CDs were observed to self-assemble onto the surface of Fe3O4 [45]. After that, the resulting mixture was magnetically separated to remove the precipitate, which was then washed with distilled water and dried at 90 °C for 4 h. This procedure was repeated using different volumes of the CDs solution (15 mL, 20 mL, 25 mL, and 30 mL) to synthesize various Fe3O4/CDs nanocomposites.

Various characterization techniques were employed to examine the physical and structural properties of Fe3O4/CDs. To identify the crystal structure and phase, x-ray diffraction (XRD) with CU-Kα radiation (Shimadzu XD-3H) was utilized. The sample's morphology was analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Jeol JEM-1400), while selected area electron diffraction (SAED) was used to study the crystal structure. Scanning electron microscopy-energy dispersive x-ray (SEM-EDX, JSM-6510LA) was used to investigate the surface morphology and the presence of nanocomposites. Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR) analysis was conducted using a Shimadzu-21 Prestige Infrared Spectrophotometer to examine the bonding of functional groups. The optical properties of the nanocomposite were evaluated using Shimadzu UV-1900 ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy and self-assembled photoluminescence (PL). Magnetic properties were measured using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, Riken Denshi Co. Ltd).

The SAR is measured using the calorimetric approach to assess the dissipation of heat energy in magnetic hyperthermia [15,39]. The SAR value of Fe3O4/CDs is determined by recording the temperature increase at a specific time interval. Preparing the nanocomposite sample involves dissolving 0.05 g in 0.15 ml of distilled water and sonication for 10 min [18,46]. The resulting solution is then placed in the center of the induction coil, as depicted in Fig. 2

The present study involved a multidisciplinary approach to synthesizing Fe3O4 nanoparticles through co-precipitation, utilizing MO leaf extract as a reducing agent and stabilizer. Additionally, CDs were synthesized hydrothermal, using watermelon rinds waste as a carbon source. The formation mechanism of Fe3O4/CDs is illustrated in Fig. 3 . In general, the formation of Fe3O4 occurs through the reduction of Fe(II) and Fe(III) ions by hydroxyl groups found in flavonoids [40]. This reduction process is followed by the nucleation phase, producing nanoparticles that merge to form a specific morphology during the growth phase. Additionally, the output of CDs involves subjecting the biologically active compounds in watermelon rinds waste to a heating process. During the fabrication process of Fe3O4/CDs, nucleation and growth processes take place, resulting in the attachment of CDs to the surface of Fe3O4 [47].

Fig. 4 illustrates the crystal structures of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/CDs, which were examined using XRD analysis. The diffraction pattern revealed the magnetite nanomaterial exhibited an inverse spinel cubic phase. The (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) crystal planes of GS-Fe3O4 were identified at 2θ angles of 30.1, 35.5, 43.4, 53.5, 57.1, and 62.80, respectively. Fe2O3 peaks appear in the XRD pattern of Fe3O4 due to oxidation during its formation at 2θ angles of 32° [48]. Furthermore, the CDs diffraction peak was identified at 2θ of 24.9°, similar to graphite characteristics (002) [47].

The Debye-Scherrer Eq. (1) is utilized to determine the size of the crystallites,
(1)
d =
k λ
B cos θ

The size of the Fe3O4 crystallite was estimated to be around 9 nm, and the incorporation of CDs resulted in a reduction in size to 8.8, 8.7, and 8.6 nm for Fe3O4/CDs 10 mL, 20 mL, and 30 mL, respectively. The reduction in crystallite size with an increasing concentration of CDs can be attributed to the strain-induced crystal distortion [49]. This distortion arises from lattice contraction during the synthesis or fabrication process. The lattice parameters of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/CDs at various concentrations were obtained using Bragg's law and found to be 8.15–8.23 Å, which comparable to the standard ICDD card no. 01–075–0033 of Fe3O4 phase reports a lattice parameter range of 8.38 Å [18,50].

Table 1 presents the findings of the phase composition analysis of Fe3O4/CDs, which was conducted using the Rietveld refinement method in the MAUD program. These reveal that surface modification decreases the Fe2O3 phase composition, signifying a reduction in oxidation as the nanoparticles are coated [51]. In this study, surface modification involved the addition of CDs, and as the concentration of CDs increased, it led to a decrease in the Fe2O3 phase composition. The results suggest that the incorporation of CDs led to modifications in the lattice parameters and a reduction in crystallite size, which indicates the presence of CDs on the surface of Fe3O4.

The fitting process also determined the cation distribution of Fe3O4 nanoparticles [52]. Before- and after-refinement parameters, including atom occupancy and atom coordination at each site are shown in Table 2 .

To examine the morphology and particle size distribution of Fe3O4/CDs, TEM microscopy analysis was conducted. Fig. 5(a) and (c) depict the TEM images of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/CDs, respectively. The nanoparticle morphology of Fe3O4/CDs and Fe3O4 are semi-spherical in shape. Fe3O4 has unclear boundaries, indicating the presence of CDs covering the surface of Fe3O4 [53]. The SAED patterns shown in Fig. 5(a) and (c) correspond to the crystallinity pattern in XRD. The particle size graph in Fig. 5 is the result of normal curve fitting using the origin program. The average particle size of Fe3O4, Fe3O4/CDs 10 mL, Fe3O4/CDs 20 mL, Fe3O4/CDs 30 mL were determined to be (9.7 ± 1.7) nm, (7.5 ± 1.9) nm, (7.4 ± 1.6) nm, and (7.2 ± 1.6) nm, respectively. The particle size value, when combined with its standard deviation, indicates a reasonable degree of agreement with the crystallite size obtained from XRD. Typically, the smaller the observed crystallite size, the smaller the corresponding particle size. In essence, these findings show a significant correlation between the particle size value and the crystallite size.

The surface topography of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/CDs with various concentrations was determined using SEM. From the illustration of Fig. 6 , it can be seen that after the addition of CDs to Fe3O4 material, CDs adhere and distribute on the surface of Fe3O4. The detection of carbon in Fe3O4/CDs provides evidence of the presence of CDs in the nanocomposite, which verifies the successful synthesis of Fe3O4/CDs.

In the presented Table 3 , it can be observed that as the concentration of CDs in the Fe3O4 increases, there is a proportional increase in the weight percentages of carbon (C) and oxygen (O), while the weight percentage of iron (Fe) decreases. This observation can be attributed to the greater incorporation of CDs onto the Fe3O4 surface, given that CDs inherently contain carbon and oxygen elements. However, in the Fe3O4/CDs 30 mL sample there are differences in results, this is likely because at the time of synthesis 30 mL CDs contain more water, so there is a bulking phenomenon where there is an increase in the volume of CDs due to their water contain [54]. This causes the percentage of Fe in Fe3O4/CDs to be greater and the percentage of O and C to be smaller than Fe3O4/CDs 20 mL.

In Fig. 7 , the FTIR spectrum of Fe3O4/CDs displays absorption bands in 500–3500 cm−1. As shown in Table 4 , typical absorption peaks for Fe-O functional groups are identified in Fe3O4 at 588.8 and 627.6 cm−1 with stretching vibration types. The stretching vibrations of flavonoids from MO were identified as the cause of the carbon absorption peaks at 1642.5 cm−1 [35]. CDs exhibit a typical absorption peak at 1642.5 cm−1 with stretching vibrations for the CC functional group. Upon adding CDs to Fe3O4/CDs, absorption peaks for CC, CO, CO-C, and OH are observed at 1642.5, 1392.5, 111.9, and 3428.4 cm−1, respectively, with different vibration types, such as stretching or bending. The OH bond of CDs is found at 3308.2 cm−1 with stretching vibrations. The shift in wave numbers after incorporating Fe3O4 and CDs is due to their interaction, indicating the successful combination of the two materials into nanocomposites [55].

UV–Vis and PL analyses were conducted to investigate the optical characteristics of Fe3O4/CDs. The results of the UV–Vis analysis indicated that Fe3O4 exhibited a peak absorption at 193 nm and a prominent peak in the range of 365–400 nm [46,56]. In contrast, CDs showed an absorption peak at 282 nm, consistent with detecting the CC functional group in the FTIR analysis. In Fe3O4/CDs, the absorption peak of Fe3O4 was observed at 193 nm, while the absorption peak of carbon was noted at 212–223 nm, indicating the successful incorporation of nanocomposites. Furthermore, it was observed that the maximum absorption peak of Fe3O4 in Fe3O4/CDs decreased as the concentration of CDs increased. Using the Tauc plot equation, the absorption peak spectrum was employed to determine the band gap energy (Eg) [57].

The Tauc plot equation, consisting of the constant A, photon energy hυ, band gap energy Eg, and a value n dependent on the transition type, was employed to compute the Eg values for Fe3O4/CDs, as tabulated in Table 5 . The determined Fe3O4 band gap value of 2.69 eV aligns with the outcomes of previous research, as conducted by Rado, A. et al. [58]. Similarly, the measured Cdots band gap value of 3.30 eV in accordance with the findings reported in the study by Kasmiarno L.D. et al. [59]. The results indicate that the Eg value increased with the rise in CDs concentration, consistent with the quantum confinement effect, implying that smaller particles exhibit higher band gap energies. This finding is in line with the TEM results.

During the PL analysis, a shift in the excitation wavelength of CDs was observed compared to Fe3O4/CDs, specifically at 509 and 505 nm, as presented in Table 6 . These wavelengths belong to the green light spectrum (495–570 nm) [60,61], which suggests that CDs emit green luminescence under UV light exposure. Furthermore, Fig. 9 illustrates that the light intensity of CDs is higher than that of Fe3O4/CDs, while Fe3O4 exhibits no intensity of emitted light. These findings highlight the significant role of CDs in determining the luminescence properties of Fe3O4/CDs.

Magnetic properties play a crucial role in magnetic hyperthermia. Fig. 10 illustrates the magnetization curves of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/CDs at room temperature, revealing that the samples are soft ferromagnetic materials [62]. The magnetization saturation (Ms) value of Fe3O4 was measured to be 54.2 emu/g, this value is less than the Fe3O4 in bulk material. This reduction may be caused by the dipole-dipole interactions which are stronger in nanoparticles with smaller interparticle average distance [63]. The magnetization saturation values decreased with increasing concentration of CDs. However, the decrease in Ms indicates that the Fe3O4/CDs nanocomposite was successfully formed. The Ms value decreases as the concentration of CDs increases due to the non-magnetic nature of CDs, which impedes the growth and movement of magnetic moments on the surface of Fe3O4 [64]. The amount of remanent magnetization depends on the saturation magnetization of the material. Therefore, the Mr value decreases as the Ms value decreases. Table 7 shows that the coercivity field value (Hc) of Fe3O4 is 55 Oe, which increases as the concentration of CDs increases. This is in line with the TEM results, indicating that as the concentration of CDs increases, the particle size decreases. According to Bance et al., smaller particles are less likely to exhibit surface defects, thus requiring higher Hc values for reverse domain nucleation [65]. Consequently, as the particle size decreases, the Hc value increases [58,66].

The efficiency of magnetic heating of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/CDs nanoparticles with varying concentrations were determined by measuring the SAR value using calorimetry. This method records the temperature increase over a certain time interval under a specific AMF strength and frequency. The SAR value can be determined by utilizing the following equation [56]:
(3)
S A R = C
(
m s
m
m n p
)
(
Δ
T
Δ
t
)

The equation for calculating the SAR value involves several parameters, including the heat capacity (C), the mass of the nanoparticles (mMNP), the mass of the solvent (ms), and the temperature gradient over time (ΔT/Δt).

This research aims to examine how the frequency, strength of AMF, and Ms impact the SAR value of nanoparticles. The temperature increase measurements were recorded every 10 s for 10 min while subjecting the nanoparticles to various AMF (100 Oe and 150 Oe) and frequency variations (10, 15, 20 kHz). The frequency and AMF variations were adjusted for safety reasons, limiting HF to less than 5 × 10−9 A/ms [18].

Fig. 11 shows the temperature versus time graph with varying AMF and frequency. The more significant the variation in AMF and frequency, the greater the temperature increase over time. However, it was observed that the larger the concentration of CDs, the smaller the temperature increase over time. This finding is consistent with the VSM results, where CDs, a non-magnetic material, decrease the saturation magnetization value of Fe3O4. The decrease in SAR value occurs because the interaction between particles after adding CDs becomes stronger, as seen from the TEM image results that show agglomeration after the addition of CDs [57,66].

Based on Eq. (3), the SAR value is proportional to the temperature increase gradient over time. Figs. 12 and 13
illustrate that the more significant the variation in CDs concentration, the smaller the SAR value obtained. Meanwhile, the more significant the variation in AMF and frequency, the greater the SAR value obtained.

In order to evaluate applicability and compare specific absorption rate (SAR) values obtained from existing literature under different extrinsic conditions including magnetic field, frequency, size, and concentration of MNPs, the more suitable term to use is intrinsic loss power (ILP, in nHm2 kg−1).
I L P
[
n H

2

g
− 1
]
=
S A R
f
H
2

Where f and H refer to the frequency and amplitude of the magnetic field. The ILP value calculated for all the samples is given in Table 8 . The ILP value of Fe3O4 nanoparticles is comparable to those of previous studies on potential magnetic hyperthermia agent. Aslibeiki, et al. reported Fe3O4 nanoparticle (F50) with an ILP value of 0.11 nHm2 kg−1 [67,68]. Fe3O4 nanoparticles have also been recently investigated by Jalili, et al. with the ILP value of 1.4 nHm2 kg−1 [69].

The main cause of the rapid temperature rise is the Nèel and Brown relaxation of each nanoparticle, which is induced by the AMF (as shown in Fig. 14 ). Hysteresis losses can add to the total heat generated during the relaxation process. However, the eddy current effect is negligible because of the nanoparticles size. Nèel relaxation pertains to the internal spin of the magnetic moment within the crystal structure of particles, whereas Brownian relaxation occurs when the particles spin aligns with the magnetic moment [70]. The introduction of CDs to the surface of Fe3O4 results in a more complex Brownian relaxation process, which causes a slight change in temperature over time, especially when the concentration of CDs is high.

Table 9 shows previous studies on magnetite based. The use of plant extracts such as MO in the synthesis of MNPs produces environmentally friendly nanoparticles. Although the SAR value obtained is relatively smaller compared to previous studies due to the use of lower frequencies and field strengths, the Fe3O4/CDs nanocomposites still have the potential as a magnetic hyperthermia agent.

The study successfully synthesized Fe3O4/CDs nanoparticles with varying concentrations of CDs via co-precipitation and hydrothermal methods utilizing MO and watermelon rinds waste as environmentally friendly materials. The XRD analysis confirmed reduced average crystallite size upon adding CDs. TEM imaging displayed fuzzy nanoparticles, indicating CDs attachment to the surface of Fe3O4. SEM-EDX mapping analysis corroborated this finding, detecting Fe, O, and C and showing the distribution of CDs on the surface of Fe3O4. The Fe3O4/CDs contained several functional groups, including FeO, CC, CO, CO-C, and OH, as indicated by the FTIR analysis. UV–Vis and PL analysis showed a decrease in the maximum absorption peak of Fe3O4 with increasing CDs concentration and the role of CDs in determining the luminescence properties of Fe3O4/CDs. The VSM analysis revealed a decrease in Ms and a widening of Hc upon CDs addition due to the non-magnetic properties of CDs. The SAR value of Fe3O4/CDs decreased as the concentration of CDs increased, and the frequency and strength of the AMF increased. These results confirm that the Ms, and the frequency and strength of AMF influence the SAR value of Fe3O4/CDs. The results amply indicate the potential utility of these agents in cancer treatment. The detailed information from this study is invaluable and will inspire researchers to further explore the development of effective magnetic nanomaterials for magnetic hyperthermia.
